Let's get into the seven pillars of your high-fee, low-stress, copywriting business. This is how we solve all those problems. Pillar number one, the three success keys. And those three keys are, first of all, a sense of importance and urgency. And what I mean by this is you need to understand the importance of what you do as a copywriter, that businesses are depending upon you to craft the words that will sell their products and services. And you have the tools, you need to get this done. And you need to have a sense of urgency. And start thinking about what is the consequence for my potential clients if I don't come along, if I don't help them create the copy that makes their business succeed. I'm robbing them of the potential increase in profits. I'm robbing their customers of their problems being solved. And I'm robbing myself and my family of our income. On all these levels, you need to have that sense of importance and urgency. The second of the three success keys is confidence. You need to approach your business with confidence. And fortunately, because you have the Storybrand framework at your disposal, you now have the confidence of knowing you always know exactly what needs to appear on a page or in an email or on a website. So for sure, you have the confidence problem handled. And the third of the three success keys is the willingness to act. You can't just sit back and think about having a copywriting business. You actually have to go talk to potential clients, create materials, sign them up and collect the fees. You have to be willing to take the action required to succeed. Pillar number two is what I call the STAR strategy. STAR is an acronym. And let's go through what the letters stand for. First is the S of STAR and it stands for specialist. And for most copywriters, this is a problem because they don't know what to specialize in, what kind of businesses to work with, how to make themselves stand out. But you are going to be a STAR because first of all, you're going to specialize in the Storybrand copywriting framework. This puts you leagues ahead of any other copywriter in the marketplace. So this problem is solved quite easily. The T in STAR stands for teacher. Now you obviously can't go out and teach Storybrand workshops, but as you work with clients you can teach them the process you're going through to create their copy, to create their Storybrand one page. This is powerful because when you are a teacher and not just a vendor, you become a respected colleague instead of just a commodity, somebody who can be called forth and dismissed. This puts you in a position of more authority. The A in STAR stands for aspiration. You want to speak to your clients both in person and on your website in your email series that you have for your clients. You want to speak to them aspirationally. Talk about their dreams, their desires, the things that they are aiming for, the goals in their business. What do they want out of this promotion or out of this website or out of this email campaign? And finally, the R of STAR stands for repetition. You continually practice these three items that I just went over. Being a specialist, being a teacher, and speaking to the aspirations of your market, your potential clients. This will make you a STAR and you will stand out. Pillar number three, you need to dimensionalize your value. Look, the first thing to understand is people don't buy copy. They buy results. They buy more sales. They buy more profits. There's a cliche in marketing that goes something like this. Nobody buys a drill. What they're buying is a hole in the wall. Well, it's kind of the same way with copy. Nobody wakes up in the morning and says, oh, what I really need is a good copywriter. What they do wake up thinking is, we need more sales in my business. We need more profits. I need somebody to help me with that. You need to start with their pain. And that is they have a great product, a great service. They don't have enough customers or the customers they have are not spending enough money with them. And something important for you to do is amplify that pain. I know this probably sounds familiar, but you need to show them as potential clients, what is the cost of not hiring you as a copywriter? And the best way you can do this is to dimensionalize or, as Jeffrey Fox calls it, dollarize your value. Ask the client, how much do you think you could potentially make with this product or service if it were selling as much as you believe it's possible to sell? Get the numbers from them. Get them to calculate what the potential profit of their business or their product or their services. Get buy-in, in other words, on the value. And then when you can have them say to you, well, I think this is a $2.5 million endeavor. It becomes much easier to get the fee you deserve because you've allowed them to arrive at stating in their terms, in their belief, what the value of your work actually is. Pillar number four is to have a marketing system. Now almost every freelance copywriter I meet does not have a marketing system. Most consultants, most coaches, heck, I mean most business people don't have a marketing system because they don't understand what a marketing system is. Running some ads capriciously on radio or in the newspaper or on Facebook, that's not a marketing system. Those are just episodes. So a marketing system is the following five things. Number one, it is defined. You can state clearly what your marketing system is, whether it is internet ads, landing pages, newspaper ads, direct mail. You can state with authority and with certainty what your system is. Number two, it is calendared. Every event in your marketing plan is on the calendar because if it's not on the calendar, let's be honest, it's not real. It's maybe a whim that you have one day and you decide to do it. But you need to have all your marketing promotions for the next quarter at least and I believe for the next year on the calendar. Number three, it is perpetual. Don't make the mistake of promoting for a while, generating a lot of business, then getting so focused on delivering on that business that you actually don't have anything in the pipeline. That's why six months later you find yourself in famine mode again. So your marketing system needs to be perpetual. Number four, I like to define a marketing system as a system for reliably producing a predictable number of affordable potential clients or partners. So let's unpack that just a little bit. You're going to know at the beginning of each month that you can rely on a certain number of potential clients coming your way. You're going to know with certainty every month that you can close a certain number of deals through your marketing. And you're going to have a pretty good idea of what they're going to spend. And you're going to know what it costs you to acquire those leads. That's what I mean by affordable. If a potential client to you is worth $5,000 on average and it costs you $1,000 in marketing to get that client, is that worth it? Well, I would trade $1,000 for $5,000 all day long. It's important that you know these numbers. And number five, your marketing system has to be strategic. In other words, you need to sit back and think in a big picture way about what is it your business as a copywriter delivers? Who do you want to work with and how do you communicate with those people? You need, in fact, a story about your business in which your clients and prospects are the hero. Fortunately, you have a framework for creating such a story about you and your business. Another number five, build the Citadel. No, I'm not talking about the school. What is a Citadel? Well, if you look it up in the dictionary, you'll learn that it's a fortress that protects the city. And what I'm going to suggest is that you build a fortress, a fortress to protect the city of your clients. What do I mean protect your clients? What are you protecting them from? You're protecting them from their own perhaps lack of focus when it comes to their marketing. You're the person who brings to their attention that they need to have a system like we were just talking about for you. Your clients need that same thing. And you are going to care about your clients. You actually feel like it means something to you if they succeed or fail. And people can sense this. It's kind of a cliche, but people don't care how much you know until they know how much you care. And when you really are emotionally invested in them being successful and them serving their clientele, you will care and they will feel that concern on your part. Finally, you need to remember the metaphor of being a shepherd. You are there to protect their interest, to shepherd them to the place where they can find food and water, and to protect them from predators. And you have the tools and the mindset and the abilities to do all five of these things. So start building your citadel for your clients today. Pillar number six is to be more. First of all, stop being a copywriter. When you go into a business and you're able to help them develop a story brand one page, you're able to help them wireframe their website. You can help them create their lead generating PDF or write their nurturing email sequence. You are so much more than just a copywriter. You need to remember that your position is different than that of other copywriters. Basically what I advise copywriters to do is create your own label for what you do. The power that you have at your disposal is the power of the story brand name that instantly puts you in a category with very few people and gives you an enormous advantage. That's why you can be more because you actually have more to offer. You have things to offer that no one else in the marketplace can offer. Only you can offer that. Next you need to get a mentor. Someone with more experience, more years, more clients under their belt than you. And someone you can call on to ask for advice when you're in a stuck place, when you're discouraged or maybe when you have a great opportunity and you just want to make sure you don't goof it up. It's good to find an experienced mentor who can help you in those moments. Finally, I suggest getting what I call a war council. These are a group of individuals that you have consciously adopted as people you turn to when you're making strategic plans, when you're getting ready to solve a problem, when you're faced with a difficulty. These are people you can call on to give you advice from a third party perspective. Sometimes when we're in a difficult spot, we don't see clearly what the next step is, but other people can look at us and very clearly instantly tell us, well, this is what you should do. Think about it. You can probably see other people's problems and solutions a lot more clearly than they can see them themselves. Pillar number seven, train your clients. What I mean by this is train them to value you as a part of their business, a part of their marketing. Train them to look at you as more than just another vendor. You are someone they can count on to make a difference in their bottom line. Increase your value that you've done that by getting this course. You need to let people know that you've done that by getting this course and you need to increase your value by continuing to practice and hone your skills. Change your venue. If you're finding it difficult to get clients who pay you the money you need or you feel you deserve or you're just not clicking with the clients that you're picking up, you change your venue by focusing on a different segment of the marketplace. Try to find the kinds of businesses that you're fascinated by, the kinds of businesses that you love to do business with. By changing your venue to people that you actually enjoy being around, you will find that they also enjoy being around you. Next, raise your fees. Now, don't go crazy and charging amounts of money that are far beyond what's appropriate for your current skill level, but do keep in mind that chances are whatever you're charging right now, you're actually worth more. Remember my experiences with quoting what I thought were big fees so that people would not hire me and remember how many times they actually said yes in an instant. It's because they have more of a sense of my value than I did. This is something for you to keep in mind as you go forward. Finally, I recommend having a shock and awe package. You already have this at your disposal. You can go to a client with a sample Storybrand One page. You can show them a sample wire frame of their website, some samples of a PDF, of nurturing email sequences. These are things that I promise you no other copywriter is going to bring to the table for these clients. They will be shocked. They will be awed and they'll be ready to engage you to do their work. All right. We've covered a lot of ground. We talked about the problems copywriters face, why those problems are even worse than they appear at first. I told you the story of how I solved these problems by accident and I gave you the answers to the problems themselves, the seven pillars of a high fee, low stress copywriting business. You now have the tools you need to enjoy the experience of copywriting as a business, to get bigger fees from fewer clients with less work and less stress. Go get them.
